sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच
मय्यासक्तमनाः पार्थ योगं युञ्जन्मदाश्रयः ।
असंशयं समग्रं मां यथा ज्ञास्यसि तच्छृणु ॥ १ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
mayy āsakta-manāḥ pārtha yogaṁ yuñjan mad-āśrayaḥ |
asaṁśayaṁ samagraṁ māṁ yathā jñāsyasi tac chṛṇu || 1 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Now hear, O son of Pṛthā, how by practicing yoga in full consciousness of Me, with mind attached to Me, you can know Me in full, free from doubt.
Now listen, O Arjun, how, with the mind attached exclusively to Me, and surrendering to Me through the practice of bhakti yog, you can know Me completely, free from doubt.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 4010 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: In this Seventh Chapter of Bhagavad-gītā, the nature of Kṛṣṇa consciousness is fully described. Kṛṣṇa is full in all opulences, and how He manifests such opulences is described herein. Also, four kinds of fortunate people who become attached to Kṛṣṇa and four kinds of unfortunate people are described.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.1 O Partha, mayi asaktamanah, having the mind fixed on Me — one whose mind (manah) is fixed (asakta) on Me (mayi) who am the supreme God possessed of the qualification going to be spoken of. Yogam yunjan, practising the Yoga of Meditation, concentrating the mind. Madasrayah, taking refuge in Me.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara emphasizes the three preconditions: mind fixed on Krishna, practice of meditational yoga, and taking refuge in him. Only with this triple posture can the promised samagram (entire) and asaṁśayam (doubtless) knowing be received.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.1 The Lord said — Listen attentively to My words imparting knowledge to you, by which you will understand Me indubitably and fully — Me, the object of the Yogic contemplation in which you are engaged with a mind so deeply bound to Me by virtue of overwhelming love.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja stresses that āsakta-manāḥ is not mere mental concentration but mind bound to Krishna through overwhelming love (prema). The Yogic contemplation Krishna refers to is bhakti-yoga, not jnana-yoga; without that bond of love the disclosure cannot land.

positions.rendering: practicing yoga in full consciousness of Me
surrendering to Me through the practice of bhakti yog
practising the Yoga of Meditation, taking refuge in Me
Yogic contemplation with mind deeply bound to Me by overwhelming love

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators name the same triple posture (mind-attached, yoga-practising, taking-refuge) but disagree on which yoga is meant. Shankara reads yogam as dhyāna-yoga (meditation) — the standard Advaita reading that 7.1 continues from 6.47's meditative yogi. Ramanuja and the Gaudiya tradition (Prabhupada, Mukundananda) read yogam as bhakti-yoga — the discipline of devotional love. The verse itself does not specify; the chapter-7 context (which opens the bhakti-heavy second hexad) supports the bhakti reading, but Shankara's continuity-with-Ch6 reading is also defensible.

literal_meaning: The Supreme Lord said: O Pārtha, with mind attached to Me, practising yoga, taking refuge in Me — hear how you will know Me without doubt and in entirety.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The opening verse of Chapter 7. Krishna shifts register: where chapters 2-6 taught technique, here he begins to reveal himself. He names three preconditions for the disclosure he is about to give — mind attached (āsakta-manāḥ), yoga practiced (yogam yuñjan), and refuge taken (mad-āśrayaḥ). With these in place, he promises a knowing of him that is samagram (entire, complete) and asaṁśayam (without doubt). The verse pivots the Gita from the technique-teaching of the first hexad into the self-revelation of the second.
